race.name: Great Bear Chase
race.tagline: Fifty-two kilometers through Michigan's Copper Country on 230 inches of annual snow — where Lake Superior's fury becomes the Upper Peninsula's gift and every finisher earns a pasty.

vitals.aid_stations: 4 feed stations with hot drinks, energy food, and cookies

climate.description: March in Michigan's Copper Country means deep lake-effect snow from Lake Superior. Annual snowfall averages 230+ inches. Temperatures range from -15°C to 0°C. The lake-effect machine guarantees snow cover when many lower-latitude venues have melted out. March brings longer days but lingering Upper Peninsula cold.
challenges: Lake-effect snow squalls can reduce visibility mid-race
Heavy snowfall can alter trail conditions overnight
Remote Upper Peninsula location with limited warming options
March sun can create slushy afternoon conditions

course.primary: A rolling loop course through the forests and hills of Michigan's Copper Country near Calumet. The terrain is gentle with moderate rollers through hardwood and conifer forests. The Keweenaw Peninsula's legendary snowfall ensures deep, consistent coverage. Post-race pasty lunch is a tradition.
course.surface: Machine-groomed trails through Copper Country forest terrain
course.grooming: Well-maintained on Copper Country trail system, benefiting from deep natural snowpack
features: 230+ inches of annual snowfall — among the snowiest venues in America
Post-race Cornish pasty lunch — a Upper Peninsula tradition
Deep community roots in Michigan's historic mining region

nordic_lab_rating.score_note: Maximum snow reliability anchored by Lake Superior's 230+ inch annual snowfall — one of the most snow-sure venues in North America. Strong community score reflects the passionate Upper Peninsula skiing culture and iconic post-race pasty tradition. Remote location limits accessibility and international draw.

history.summary: Founded in 1981, the Great Bear Chase has become the premier ski marathon of Michigan's Upper Peninsula. Set in the Copper Country near Calumet — a region with deep Finnish-American heritage and 230+ inches of annual snowfall — the race celebrates the legendary snow culture of the Keweenaw Peninsula. The post-race Cornish pasty lunch honors the mining heritage of a region where Finnish, Cornish, and Italian immigrants built a skiing tradition.
notable_facts: Founded in 1981 in Michigan's historic Copper Country
230+ inches of average annual snowfall from Lake Superior lake-effect
Post-race Cornish pasty lunch is a beloved tradition
Calumet has deep Finnish-American heritage and skiing culture
The Keweenaw Peninsula is one of the snowiest places in America east of the Rockies
Named after the bears that inhabit the surrounding forests

youtube_data.search_query: Great Bear Chase cross country ski race Calumet

videos.title: Cross country skiers hit the trails for Great Bear Chase
videos.description: Calumet was the place to be this past weekend for the Great Bear Chase Ski Marathon. ABC 10 Keweenaw Bureau reporter Sam Ali was there for a complete recap of the event.

videos.title: Organizers prepare for annual Great Bear Chase
videos.channel: Upper Michigan's Source | TV6 & FOX UP
videos.description:
Organizers prepare for annual Great Bear Chase

For more Local News from WLUC: https://www.uppermichiganssource.com/
For more YouTube Content: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC0VdQ0qx4RpethtRWqjkSCA
videos.transcript:
The copper dog may have concluded last
weekend, but the copper country has more
winter activities to check out. A racing
event is nearly ready to begin in
Calumet.
>> TV6's Hoten Hancock Bureau reporter
Colin Jackson tells us how many racers
are involved this year and just how much
of an impact this race has on the area.
>> So far, we have 842
who have signed up. That seems oddly
specific, but I just looked. Hundreds of
participants are ready and raring to
race in the latest Great Bear Chase
skiing event this weekend. From the 52
km freestyle to the 1 kilometer junior
bear chase, these racers will be
competing across a variety of distances
to cross the finish line. First,
organizing committee member Dean
Woodbeck says the appeal of the event
comes down to three factors.
>> One is uh we almost always have snow. In
recent years in the upper Midwest,
sometimes we're the only place with
snow. We've become known for our
volunteers being very friendly and we
create kind of a friendly atmosphere.
And then I think the third thing is our
organizing committee. We've got a broad
range of people. So we have people who
ski race a lot. We have people like me
who don't ski race a lot.
>> Wood Beck also says that he and fellow
organizers have been keeping an eye on
how much economic impact the event has
on the area for the past four years.
>> We contribute something like $700,000 to
the economy in this event. you know,
just from Friday night through Sunday.
>> Race director Angela Luskin says the
event needs 150 volunteers each year to
run smoothly. She says all necessary
volunteer positions have been filled,
but encourages anyone still interested
in racing to register before it's too
late.
>> Online registration closes Thursday
night at midnight and then we'll have
last minute registration at um the Calat
High School from 4:30 to 8:30 p.m. on
Friday. No race day registration, so
Friday is the deadline. The races kick
off Saturday morning at 8 with the 52 km
skiathlon with the starting line located
near Spruce Street in Calat. Organizers
recommend planning for a 15minute walk
to the race start. Colin Jackson, TV6
News, Calumet.

videos.title: Great Bear Chase 2014 - 10k course
videos.description: New for 2014 the 10k course at the Great Bear Chase Ski Marathon in Calumet, MI gets skiers out on the same beautiful Swede Town trails the 25 and 50k course use.  If you are looking to give nordic ski racing a try, this a great course to start on.  Get Video+GPS or the course at: http://trailgenius.com/trail-genius-map/great-bear-chase-10k-2014
videos.curation_reason: Complete POV course footage showing the 10k route through Swede Town trails for course preview.

videos.title: Goldilocks Spring Skiing: The Great Bear Chase (The Crossing Place 2025)
videos.description:
In the Keweenaw, the joys of cross-country skiing extend into early spring, thanks to the hundreds of inches of snow that fall each season! Since 1981, the Great Bear Chase has welcomed skiers from around the world to Calumet, Michigan, to experience the Copper Country's excellent late-season trail conditions in this friendly competition. While many other places in the Great Lakes region turn green by March, the UP Health Great Bear Chase Marathon offers skiers a variety of races and distances to enjoy!

___________________________________________________________________________

"The Crossing Place" is a Visit Keweenaw documentary film series that explores the deep veins of inspiration and majesty that run through the people, organizations, and experiences that make the Keweenaw truly singular. Explore more of the Crossing Place at: https://www.visitkeweenaw.com/the-crossing-place/

___________________________________________________________________________

Sign up for The Great Bear Chase or learn more about the annual race here: https://www.greatbearchase.com/

___________________________________________________________________________

To learn more about Visit Keweenaw, and find your reason to #AdventureNorth, visit: www.visitkeweenaw.com

videos.title: Challenge Accepted: 25K X Country Skiing Race... Just Learned to Ski...
videos.description:
Michigan's Great Bear Chase is a gathering of graceful, well-trained athletes who breeze their way through nature's bliss, hardened muscles rippling under their cute little bibs as they propel their way into greatness.

There was also me. I was totally there... enjoying my second week of cross country skiing ever... Challenge accepted!

Let me know in the comments below what challenge you'd like to see next!

videos.title: Great Bear Chase 50km Ski Marathon 2025
videos.description:
Very fun race. Would highly recommend.
Ski from March 8th, 2025

Event info: https://www.greatbearchase.com/
videos.transcript:
The Great Bear Chase is a ski marathon
held on the Swede Town Trail, which are
my local trails. Now, I'm not a good
skier at all, but I thought this just
sounded like fun, so I signed up for the
50K the night before. This was my first
cross country ski race ever and my 15th
ever time on cross country skis. I
definitely wasn't there to compete, but
it just sounded like a fun challenge to
me. Uh, so my longest ski up to this
point was wasn't even 15 mi long. I
wasn't entirely sure that I had what it
took to finish, but wanted to give it a
good shot. So, I set out with three main
goals. I wanted to finish before the
6-hour cut off. Uh, I didn't want to
break anything cuz I didn't want to have
to buy new poles or skis. I didn't want
to fall because I've fallen almost every
time I've been out skate skiing. And,
you know, always try to not do
that. My technique is not good. So, I
was trying to do my best to just stay
out of the way of the people who are
actually here to race.
Just by watching the people ski in front
of me, feel like I'm finally starting to
get the hang of this skate skiing
thing. It's starting to come together
after my technique still sucks, but it's
improving every kilometer.
Man, this is
fun. I don't know if I'll be saying that
the second time around here,
but for now, this is cool.
I like skiing with all these people
here, watching them, getting passed by
all the classic skiers.
Got about 8K till 25K to go. So, got a
while left,
but I'm feeling okay.
I'm good.
time on your right.
Oh man, no flamingo hill today.
on your
left. Go. Go up here. 50k. Yep.
Just 25k to go.
Easy. This is my first time ever skiing
25k and I'm feeling it. My hips and feet
and
shoulders,
man. Going uphill. Almost broke a
pole. Getting sloppy. My technique was
getting sloppy. The trail conditions
were still perfect, but I'd consider
that a fall. So, there goes my goal not
to fall.
Oh man.
Such a nice day for
this. Almost broke a pole again. Stuck
it on the inside of my ski and tripped
over it, but didn't break it. So, still
have the possibility for two
stars. Just got a beat cut off and not
break
anything.
So, I don't know.
If I'm even at risk of cut off, I have
no clue what time it
is. Doesn't really matter. I'm just
going to I got this pace. Can't go much
faster. So, this is uh the pace I'm
going.
That was a close one. Oh, I tweaked my
back. Oh, my back hurts like hell. I'm
getting old. What the
hell, man? I am
uh hurting. My back is in
pain. My shoulders are dead.
But feet are killing me. Hips are almost
gone. Aside from that, I'm feeling
great. I'm definitely going to finish.
Just uh not
fast. I'm only using the poles when
necessary. My
shoulders, they're gone.
5k to
go and do another 5k.
3k to
go. Should empty the tank if it's not
already
empty. Final
kilometer.
Woohoo. I ended up finishing in 3 hours
32 minutes, which is way faster than I
expected. But 11 people over the age of
60 beat me, which is way less than I
expected, but still insane. So, I hope
one day I'm able to get to that shape.
Hopefully before I'm
60. I did fall twice, but didn't break
anything. So, I'm pretty happy with
that. It's just a really fun time out on
the trails with perfect conditions.
I'm not sure if I'm going to enter any
other ski races after this, but I'm
definitely going to keep skiing for fun
because it's just a good time. If you
haven't tried it, say give it a
go. Then afterwards, I made my way to
the high school for a free pasti. Pretty
great day.
videos.curation_reason: Excellent first-person POV race recap from a beginner's perspective with detailed course commentary and personal experience.
